summary: Two material doctrine claims in `docs/architecture.md` still overstate what the code actually does: the `dynamic` rule is stricter than the current routes, and the atomic-write exemplar list incorrectly includes `reconcile.ts`.

findings.title: `dynamic = "force-dynamic"` is described as required for live composition reads, but `compositions/[id]` reads live composition state without it
findings.recommendation: Relax the rule or document the real exception set. As written, this says `dynamic` is required whenever a route reads live disk/composition state, but `src/app/api/compositions/[id]/route.ts` serves `readCompositionWithDerivedTasks(params.id)` from a live composition file and only exports `runtime = "nodejs"`. A generic implementer would be misled into thinking the repo uniformly follows a stronger caching rule than it actually does.

findings.title: The host-config IO section incorrectly says `reconcile.ts` writes through `atomic-write.ts`
findings.recommendation: Remove `reconcile.ts` from the “all of which write through `src/lib/atomic-write.ts`” list, or narrow the statement. `src/lib/reconcile.ts` writes captured fittings under `capturedFittingsDir()` (`~/.garrison/fittings`) via `fsp.cp(...)` and `writeYamlFile(...)`, and `src/lib/yaml.ts` uses raw `fs.writeFile`, not the atomic helper. Leaving `reconcile.ts` in this list misstates the actual IO discipline a new implementer would find in code.
